finding: Local MaaS high-unlock-fee penalty survives every leave-one-platform-city check
evidence: For EUR 0.99, EUR 1.00, and EUR 1.20 thresholds, local MaaS interactions are negative and significant in 13/13 city-drop models under both market FE and unit FE. Median market-FE coefficients are -0.199, -0.209, and -0.218; median unit-FE coefficients are -0.044, -0.035, and -0.038.
interpretation: The local MaaS high-unlock-fee penalty is not driven by a single platform city; it persists when each major platform city is removed one at a time.
caveat: The local MaaS treatment remains city-structured, but no individual city explains the result.

finding: Multi-platform high-unlock-fee penalty is stable after city drops at EUR 1.00 and EUR 1.20
evidence: For EUR 1.00 and EUR 1.20 thresholds, multi-platform interactions are negative and significant in 13/13 city-drop models under both market FE and unit FE. Median market-FE coefficients are -0.107 and -0.135; median unit-FE coefficients are -0.085 and -0.073.
interpretation: The multi-platform high-unlock-fee penalty is not a single-city artifact at the most salient high-fee thresholds.
caveat: The EUR 0.99 threshold is directionally stable but less significant in market FE for some city drops.

finding: The EUR 0.99 multi-platform threshold is directionally robust but weaker in market FE
evidence: For EUR 0.99, multi-platform coefficients remain negative in all 13 city-drop market-FE models, but only 9/13 are significant; unit FE is negative and significant in 13/13 city drops.
interpretation: The lower high-fee threshold already points in the expected direction, but the more defensible multi-platform claim starts at EUR 1.00.
caveat: Use EUR 1.00 and EUR 1.20 as cleaner paper cutoffs.
